I am not saying there shouldn't be a 79 characters limit for other languages, just really annoyed with that limit for languages forcing you to indent at every declaration or loop such as Python. It makes it really easy to pass that number of characters if you have explicit variable and function names.

For example with a 4 space characters indentation, just declaring a class, making a function and having a try catch with a loop or condition and you are already left with 63 characters per line. If on top of that you have a couple explicit names you reach quickly that limit.

In other languages where indentation is not forced on you it's much easier to deal with.
This is of course my personal opinion about Python, flake8 and the 79 characters limitation.

* ship a release candidate with only this patch so people can test it and we can collect feedbacks

After trying to devise some test cases, I think the desired behavior is to use the mtime of the target (here, os.stat follows all of the links in the chain).  I could think of the following cases:

1. If you modify the referent file, the target's mtime will change, causing a refresh.
2. If you change the name of the link, you'll get a refresh because the filename is part of the hash.  Also, the mtime of the containing directory will change.
3. If you change the target of the link to a different file, the target's mtime will change.  Also, the mtime of the containing directory will change.
4. If there is a link to another link which changes in such a way as to point to the same file, we should NOT refresh, as the interceding links have no relevance on the content.

Additionally in case (4), the max of the mtime of the link and the target would not have changed either, making the two approaches equivalent.

Correct; the idea is to transfer links that would break; this would fall under case 8.  I agree that it's a pathological example to have a link like that, but nothing stops someone from doing something silly like that.

I agree that it's a lot of trouble to protect people from this pathological use case, but I don't think there's a huge computational burden.  It would be my preference to fail early, when possible, rather than failing when trying to reference the link.  Should we put some debugging statements for when links are dereferenced (and if so, what's the "DNAnexus way" of doing so)?

Iterator in Scala is not something that a) guarantees to provide size (it can be unknown), b) is usable after sizing (TraversableOnce, e.g.) c) it may not even have a size.

If you have an iterator, using size may be destructive. If you know the size ahead, and it's not heavy, it may be better to use a different type. Seq, for instance.

As we discussed, my recommendation would be to make each of these a unique, parameter-less tag defined either in core's `kolibri_tags.py` (e.g. `bootstrap_session`) or in a particular plugin's tag list (e.g. `bootstrap_recommended_content` in Learn).

Internally, each of these tags would be a thin wrapper around helper functions similar to `kolibri_bootstrap_collection` and `kolibri_bootstrap_model`, essentially just passing in the right parameters.

This has a few advantages:

* gives each bootstrap command a name, making it easier to scan and read
* takes complex logic out of django templates and puts it into real python files
* gets rid of the hacky `kwargs_` prefix-based interface

So... this is why I had asked you to take a look at the release notes in the PR, before I submitted it and made the official 1.5.0 tag.  I can make some edits this once, but in general I don't want to be in the position of wordsmithing the release notes after the official release goes out and creating divergence in the release notes content across branches, unless there's an actual error or crucial omission.

Re: the contributor linking, GitHub doesn't interpret GitHub-specific markdown links (issue numbers, @ handles) within ordinary markdown file views since it doesn't know whether that file actually has any connection to GitHub per se (other than being hosted on it).  The @ handles do get interpreted within the actual Code > Releases page on GitHub.  Personally, I think that amount of linking is sufficient and that having to manually construct the handle links just to get it to show up in RELEASE.md is both extra toil and makes the file significantly less readable as raw markdown.  For comparison, tensorFlow's relnotes just use contributor names and don't link to them at all.

Re: by vs from, I think that's down in the weeds.  The important thing is giving credit, not the precise word we use to do it.

Re: links at the ends of sentences, I thought it was useful to show the exact issue for sub-features, but if you prefer I can keep issue links all at the end of the line.  I'd rather stick with parens than colons though since parens are what we already have been using and they're what GitHub automatically does with submitted PRs so it requires less manual editing of the commit messages when I copy them over.

I'm a bit confused about why this needs to go through the worker at all. If I understand correctly, the following happens:

1. A local scheduler assigns a task to a worker.
2. The actor takes the last object ID from the task spec and calls that the dummy object ID.
3. The actor passes the dummy object ID back to the local scheduler in `execution_returns`.
4. The local scheduler designates that object as locally available.

Since all of the information is in the local scheduler to begin with, couldn't we just bypass the actor entirely and have the local scheduler do the bookkeeping for itself?

Checking for length of the value of the last `TextElement` would mean that we create the element, add spans to it in `with_span` and then discard it. Returning `None` would in turn create a precedent where we need to check the truthiness of nodes returned from `get_` methods, at least in `with_span`.

Another reason to not optimize this code wrt. duplication right now is that a lot of it might change when we fix #15. If it then turns out it's easier to return `None` after all, we can add it.
